sentenceStructure.openingStyle: Bold declarative statement or contrarian take
sentenceStructure.paragraphPattern: Single-sentence paragraphs with heavy line breaks for mobile optimization. Every 1-3 sentences gets a break.

avoidList: at the end of the day
signaturePhrases: But here's what I tell them:
This is exactly why we built...
The future of marketing isn't... It's...
Which side of history will you be on?
The fear is real. But so is the opportunity.
Here is the problem with [X] in a nutshell.
The hiring process is totally broken.
AI has fundamentally changed this equation.

rhetoric.hookPattern: Bold declarative statement, contrarian take, or customer quote opener
rhetoric.ctaStyle: Challenge question (Which side? Which one are you?) or advantage statement (Companies that figure this out first...)

long.description: Extended essays with frameworks and templates

canonicalExamples.text:
Actual quote from a CEO on a call today:

"We spent $47K in AI marketing tool licenses and not only isn't performance better, we feel even more bloated than ever with all these tools."
canonicalExamples.why: Customer quote opener -- highest engagement post (1,040 reactions). Grounds the post in a real conversation with specific dollar amount.

canonicalExamples.text:
What I learned from selling my first company:

In 2017, we sold Bucketfeet to Threadless.

7 years of building, pivoting, grinding.

Here's what that experience taught me:

1. Exits rarely look like you imagined
2. The journey matters more than the destination
3. Your team is your legacy
4. Relationships opened every door
5. The next thing is always unclear until it isn't

After Bucketfeet, I took time off.

Then I met up with Chris Toy, an old friend from college.

We started exploring ideas.

One of those ideas became MarketerHire.

Now we've helped 6,000+ companies find great marketers.

The entrepreneurial journey is rarely linear.

But if you keep building, keep learning, keep connecting...

Good things tend to happen.
canonicalExamples.why: Personal journey post -- second highest engagement (423 reactions, 76 comments). Shows vulnerability, numbered lessons, inspirational closer, and natural bridge to MarketerHire.

canonicalExamples.text:
Hot take: Most companies don't have a marketing problem.

They have a product problem.

Or a positioning problem.

Or a market problem.

Marketing can amplify what's working.

But it can't fix fundamental issues with the business.

I've seen companies pour millions into marketing with nothing to show for it.

Not because the marketing was bad.

But because they were trying to market something that didn't resonate.

Before you hire marketers or buy tools, ask yourself:

- Do customers actually want what we're selling?
- Is our positioning clear and differentiated?
- Are we in a market that can support our goals?

If the answer to any of these is 'no', fix that first.

Then marketing can do its job.
canonicalExamples.why: Contrarian take pattern -- strong engagement (267 reactions, 54 comments). Shows the 'Hot take:' opener, single-sentence paragraph pacing, diagnostic bullet list, and practical closer.

canonicalExamples.text:
The generalist SaaS era is ending. The specialist system era is beginning.

Which side of history will you be on?
canonicalExamples.why: Signature challenge-question closer -- creates binary choice that drives comments and self-identification.
Bold emotional declaration opener -- three words that create immediate curiosity and drove highest comment count (87 comments).
Signature self-aware dismount -- provides humor and humility after a strong take, very distinctive to Raaja's voice.

openingPatterns.pattern: Definitive statement about an industry shift or reality. Often a single sentence.
examples: We're witnessing the end of SaaS as we know it.
The era of "off-the-shelf" marketing strategy is over.
CMOs are terrified right now.
We have a new problem in marketing: Speed without soul.

openingPatterns.pattern: Direct identification of a specific problem in accessible language.
examples: Here is the problem with hiring in a nutshell.
Why do startups struggle with marketing?
The most underrated skill in marketing: patience.

openingPatterns.pattern: Signals challenging conventional wisdom with a label followed by the contrarian claim.
examples: Hot take: Most companies don't have a marketing problem.
Unpopular opinion: Most marketing agencies are fundamentally broken.
Time to say the secret out loud.

openingPatterns.pattern: Leads with a real quote from a specific role and context, grounding the post in reality.
examples:
Actual quote from a CEO on a call today:

"We spent $47K in AI marketing tool licenses and not only isn't performance better, we feel even more bloated than ever with all these tools."

openingPatterns.pattern: Shares from his own journey to establish credibility and connection.
examples: I've been an entrepreneur for 15 years now.
Building MarketerHire has taught me one thing above all else:
What I learned from selling my first company:
Had dinner with 20 LA founders last night.

openingPatterns.pattern: Creates perspective through a sequence of historical events, building to a pivot.
examples:
Google was founded in 1998.

LinkedIn was founded in 2002.

Facebook was founded in 2004.

The iPhone was released in 2007.

Instagram was founded in 2010.

All of these changed marketing forever.

But none of them compare to what AI is doing right now.

openingPatterns.pattern: Shares an insight from direct conversations or working with many people, citing specific numbers.
examples: I've talked to dozens of them over the past few months. The common thread? Fear.
I've worked with thousands of marketers over the past 6 years building MarketerHire.
I think because marketing is so approachable, people think it's easy.

Pretty excited to share:

1. We crossed 6,000 customers earlier this year at MarketerHire
2. Our newest product MH-1 is now live and converting

What a year.

placementRules: Raaja uses virtually no emojis across all 20 posts analyzed
Zero emojis is the default and expected pattern
Maximum 1 emoji in rare cases, strategically placed

structuralPatterns.name: Bold Claim > Context > 'Here's the thing:' Pivot > Explanation > MarketerHire Bridge > Bulleted Features > Challenge Closer
structure: 'Here's the thing:' pivot to core insight

structuralPatterns.name: Customer Quote > Paradox Label > Problem List > Insight > Problem Stack > Solution Bridge > Simple Truth Closer
structure: Direct customer quote with role and context
structuralPatterns.useCase: Customer pain point / product positioning posts

structuralPatterns.name: Contrarian Opener > Real Problem Diagnosis > Concrete Example > Self-Check Questions > Conditional Advice
structure: Explanation of the real underlying problem

structuralPatterns.name: Personal Experience > Credentials > Numbered Lessons > Reflection > Inspirational Closer
structure: List of companies or experiences for credibility

structuralPatterns.name: Emotional State Opener > Credibility > Fear/Problem List > 'But here's what I tell them:' Reframe > Advice > Solution Bridge > Binary Choice Closer
structure: Emotional state of audience ('CMOs are terrified')
'But here's what I tell them:' reframe

structuralPatterns.name: Contrasting Lists (Good vs Average)
structure: Statement about AI widening the gap

neverUse: Long dense paragraphs without line breaks
Lengthy introductions before getting to the point
Questions without providing his own answer
at the end of the day
formattingToAvoid: Dense paragraphs without line breaks -- always use single-sentence paragraphs
Excessive formatting (bold, headers, etc.) -- keep it clean text
Inline hashtags -- no hashtags at all
Emoji-heavy posts -- zero or max 1 emoji
Posts without at least one list (bullet or numbered)
toneToAvoid: Claiming expertise he does not have
Making claims without numbers when numbers are available
Hedging language ('I think maybe...' 'It could be...')

hashtagStyle.format: N/A -- Raaja almost never uses hashtags

patterns: Mentions companies by name (MarketerHire, Bucketfeet, Threadless)
Mentions co-founder Chris Toy by name in narrative context
Does not do heavy @mention tagging of other people
References roles generically ('CMOs', 'a CEO on a call today')
specialPhrases: We've fixed this (for marketing at least) at MarketerHire.
This is exactly why we built MarketerHire differently.
